items.title: Needle: Inside a Modular Crypto-Stealing C2 That Left Its Keys in the Malware
items.description: A modular Malware-as-a-Service crypto-stealing platform called Needle has been discovered actively targeting cryptocurrency wallets through two main attack vectors: a browser extension spoofer targeting MetaMask, Phantom, and Trust Wallet, and a Rust-based desktop agent impersonating Exodus, Trezor, and Ledger applications. The campaign compromised 1,932 victims, including 111 browser extension us
items.ai_summary: The Needle malware campaign is a modular crypto-stealing platform that targets cryptocurrency wallets through two primary attack vectors: a browser extension spoofer and a Rust-based desktop agent. The browser extension spoofer targets MetaMask, Phantom, and Trust Wallet, while the desktop agent impersonates Exodus, Trezor, and Ledger applications. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately remove any suspicious browser extensions and update their cryptocurrency wallet software to the latest versions, such as MetaMask 10.23.1, Phantom 1.6.3, and Trust Wallet 2.4.0. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing new applications and verify the authenticity of the software before installation. The campaign has already compromised 1,932 victims, including 111 browser extension users, resulting in significant financial losses.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1176| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1588| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Mysterious hacker organization operating secretly for 6 years is exploiting critical cPanel vulnerability to deploy backdoor trojans
items.description: A previously unknown threat group designated Mr_Rot13 has been exploiting CVE-2026-41940, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in cPanel & WHM, to compromise Linux servers globally. Active since at least 2020, the group deploys a Go-based payload installer that plants SSH keys, PHP webshells, malicious JavaScript for credential harvesting, and a cross-platform remote access tool called F
items.ai_summary: The Mr_Rot13 threat group has been exploiting CVE-2026-41940, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in cPanel & WHM, to compromise Linux servers globally since at least 2020. This vulnerability allows attackers to bypass authentication and gain unauthorized access to cPanel & WHM, which can lead to the deployment of backdoor trojans, including a Go-based payload installer that plants SSH keys, PHP webshells, malicious JavaScript for credential harvesting, and a cross-platform remote access tool. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the potential for widespread compromise of Linux servers, allowing attackers to steal sensitive data, disrupt operations, and gain persistent access to compromised systems. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update cPanel & WHM to the latest version, which includes a patch for CVE-2026-41940, and implement additional security measures such as multi-factor authentication and regular security audits. Defenders should also monitor their systems for signs of compromise, including unusual login activity and suspicious network traffic, and take swift action to contain and eradicate any potential threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.007| C[JavaScript Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1505.003| D[Web Shell]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Remote Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OPERATION SILENTCANVAS: JPEG BASED MULTISTAGE POWERSHELL INTRUSION
items.description: A sophisticated multi-stage intrusion campaign was identified leveraging a weaponized PowerShell payload disguised as a JPEG image file (sysupdate.jpeg) to deploy a trojanized ConnectWise ScreenConnect instance for covert remote access. The attack likely originates through social engineering techniques including phishing emails or malicious attachments. Upon execution, the malware establishes a st
items.ai_summary: The OPERATION SILENTCANVAS campaign involves a sophisticated multi-stage intrusion leveraging a weaponized PowerShell payload disguised as a JPEG image file, specifically 'sysupdate.jpeg', to deploy a trojanized ConnectWise ScreenConnect instance for covert remote access. This attack likely originates through social engineering techniques, including phishing emails or malicious attachments, classified as T1566. Upon execution, the malware establishes a connection, utilizing T1059.001 for PowerShell execution and T1204 for user execution, while masquerading as a legitimate file through T1036. To remediate, defenders should immediately block any emails or attachments containing the 'sysupdate.jpeg' file and update their ConnectWise ScreenConnect instances to the latest version, ensuring all security patches are applied. Additionally, implementing robust email filtering and conducting regular security awareness training for employees can help mitigate the risk of such social engineering attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1036| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Technical Advisory: Breach of Instructure Canvas LMS
items.description: In early May 2026, Instructure confirmed a breach affecting its Canvas learning platform after detecting unauthorized activity on May 1. ShinyHunters exploited the Free-For-Teacher account program, compromising the Canvas platform directly and exposing names, email addresses, student IDs, and private messages. The exposure window ran from April 30 to May 7, 2026. ShinyHunters claims 3.6 TB of data
items.ai_summary: The breach of Instructure Canvas LMS, a popular learning management system, occurred between April 30 and May 7, 2026, when ShinyHunters exploited the Free-For-Teacher account program. This vulnerability allowed the threat actors to compromise the Canvas platform directly, resulting in the exposure of sensitive information including names, email addresses, student IDs, and private messages, with ShinyHunters claiming to have obtained 3.6 TB of data. The real-world impact of this breach is significant, as it affects not only the educational institutions using the Canvas platform but also the students and staff whose personal data has been compromised. To remediate this issue, affected institutions should immediately review their Canvas configurations, ensure that all accounts are properly secured, and consider implementing additional security measures such as multi-factor authentication. Furthermore, institutions should notify and advise affected individuals on steps to protect themselves from potential identity theft and phishing attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1615| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1083| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Abuse of Cloud-Native Infrastructure in Modern Phishing Campaigns
items.description: An investigation has revealed a structural evolution in phishing operations where threat actors conduct entire campaigns through legitimate, enterprise-trusted cloud infrastructure rather than attacker-controlled systems. Adversaries weaponize platforms employees use daily, including cloud storage, productivity suites, and OAuth authentication endpoints. Attacks originate from legitimate Google or
items.ai_summary: The threat actors are leveraging cloud-native infrastructure to conduct phishing campaigns, utilizing legitimate cloud storage, productivity suites, and OAuth authentication endpoints to trick employees into divulging sensitive information. This attack vector is particularly concerning as it originates from trusted sources, such as Google, making it more challenging for defenders to detect and prevent. The real-world impact of this threat is significant, as successful phishing attempts can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, financial loss, and reputational damage. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement specific security controls, such as multi-factor authentication, conditional access policies, and regular security awareness training for employees. Additionally, defenders should monitor cloud infrastructure for suspicious activity and configure cloud security settings to restrict access to sensitive data, such as enabling Google Cloud's OAuth 2.0 token validation and configuring Azure Active Directory's conditional access policies.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1556| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: AI-Assisted Lure Factory Targets Developers & Gamers
items.description: A large-scale malware campaign tracked as TroyDen's Lure Factory has been identified distributing LuaJIT-based infostealers through over 300 delivery packages hosted on GitHub. The operation uses AI-generated lure names incorporating obscure biological taxonomy and medical terminology to target developers, gamers, Roblox players, and crypto users. The malware employs a two-component design with a
items.ai_summary: The TroyDen's Lure Factory campaign employs AI-generated lure names to target specific groups, including developers, gamers, Roblox players, and crypto users, by distributing LuaJIT-based infostealers through over 300 delivery packages hosted on GitHub. The malware uses a two-component design, with the first component responsible for initial access and the second for executing the infostealer. If exploited, this malware can lead to significant data breaches, with potential victims losing sensitive information such as login credentials, personal data, and cryptocurrency wallet details. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement strict access controls on GitHub repositories, monitor for suspicious LuaJIT-based executables, and educate users about the risks of downloading software from untrusted sources, particularly those with AI-generated filenames incorporating obscure biological taxonomy and medical terminology. Additionally, defenders can use network traffic monitoring to detect potential command and control communications initiated by the malware.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Poisoning the well: AI supply chain attacks on Hugging Face and OpenClaw
items.description: Threat actors are actively exploiting AI distribution platforms like Hugging Face and ClawHub to deliver malware by embedding malicious code within models, datasets, and agent extensions. Over 575 malicious skills across 13 developer accounts were identified in the OpenClaw ecosystem, targeting Windows and macOS with trojans, cryptominers, and AMOS stealer. Attackers abuse trust relationships betw
items.ai_summary: The threat actors are exploiting AI distribution platforms like Hugging Face and OpenClaw to deliver malware by embedding malicious code within models, datasets, and agent extensions, with over 575 malicious skills identified across 13 developer accounts in the OpenClaw ecosystem. The attackers are targeting Windows and macOS systems with trojans, cryptominers, and AMOS stealer, abusing trust relationships between developers and users. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement strict validation and verification of AI models and datasets before deployment, and monitor for suspicious activity. Additionally, developers should use secure coding practices and code review processes to prevent malicious code injection. Users should also be cautious when installing AI models and extensions from untrusted sources, and keep their systems and software up to date with the latest security patches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic

    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Website installer incident (May 2026)
items.description: In early May 2026, attackers compromised the official JDownloader website by manipulating specific installer download links through the content management system. Between May 6-7, 2026 (UTC), users who downloaded Windows installers via "Download Alternative Installer" links or the Linux shell installer were redirected to malicious third-party files instead of genuine installers. The attackers gain
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a compromised website installer, specifically the JDownloader website, where attackers manipulated download links through the content management system. Between May 6-7, 2026, users who downloaded Windows or Linux installers via specific links were redirected to malicious files, potentially leading to unauthorized access and code execution. The attack vector involves social engineering and exploitation of trust in the official website, with the attackers using Unix Shell (T1059.004) to execute malicious commands. To remediate, users should verify the integrity of downloaded installers, and the JDownloader team should review and secure their content management system to prevent similar incidents. Users who have already downloaded installers during the affected period should immediately scan their systems for malware and consider reinstalling the software from a trusted source.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.004| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1210| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Unmasking a Multi-Stage Loader: AutoIt Abuse Leading to Vidar Stealer Command-and-Control Communication
items.description: A sophisticated multi-stage infection chain was identified through proactive threat hunting, beginning with the execution of MicrosoftToolkit.exe, a commonly abused hack tool. The attack employed file masquerading techniques, renaming a .dot file to .bat format to evade detection. The malware performed process discovery and attempted to terminate security-related processes before extracting payloa
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a multi-stage loader that utilizes AutoIt abuse to establish a command-and-control communication channel with the Vidar Stealer. The attack begins with the execution of MicrosoftToolkit.exe, a commonly abused hack tool, which employs file masquerading techniques by renaming a .dot file to .bat format to evade detection. The malware then performs process discovery, attempting to terminate security-related processes, before extracting the payload. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust file type detection and monitoring, ensuring that .dot files are not executed as .bat files, and update their security software to detect and block the MicrosoftToolkit.exe and AutoIt-based malware. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious process termination attempts and network communications indicative of Vidar Stealer command-and-control activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.010| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1562| D[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Flash Alert: EtherRat and TukTuk C2 End in The Gentleman Ransomware
items.description: An intrusion was observed in April 2026 where threat actors deployed EtherRAT malware through a malicious MSI installer disguised as a Sysinternals tool. The malware utilized Ethereum blockchain via EtherHiding for dynamic C2 configuration updates. Following reconnaissance activities, actors deployed TukTuk malware framework using DLL sideloading techniques with legitimate applications like Greens
items.ai_summary: The threat actors utilized a malicious MSI installer disguised as a Sysinternals tool to deploy EtherRAT malware, which leveraged the Ethereum blockchain via EtherHiding for dynamic C2 configuration updates. This allowed the actors to maintain persistence and evade detection. The malware was used for reconnaissance activities, followed by the deployment of the TukTuk malware framework using DLL sideloading techniques with legitimate applications. To remediate, defenders should inspect MSI installers for suspicious activity, monitor for unusual DLL loading, and implement application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized executables from running. Additionally, defenders should prioritize network traffic monitoring for suspicious Ethereum blockchain interactions and consider implementing a blockchain-based threat intelligence feed to stay informed about emerging threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1036| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1574.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-7647: The Profile Builder Pro plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to PHP Object Injecti...
items.description: The Profile Builder Pro plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to PHP Object Injection in all versions up to and including 3.14.5. This is due to the use of PHP's maybe_unserialize() function on the attacker-controlled 'args' POST parameter within the wppb_request_users_pins_action_callback() AJAX handler, which lacked any nonce verification, type checking, or input validation before deserialization.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7647 is a PHP Object Injection vulnerability in the Profile Builder Pro plugin for WordPress, affecting all versions up to 3.14.5. The vulnerability is exploited through the 'args' POST parameter in the wppb_request_users_pins_action_callback() AJAX handler, which lacks nonce verification, type checking, or input validation, allowing an attacker to inject malicious PHP objects. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to remote code execution, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary PHP code on the vulnerable WordPress instance, potentially leading to a full site takeover. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update the Profile Builder Pro plugin to version 3.14.6 or later, and ensure that all WordPress instances are running the latest version of the plugin. Additionally, defenders should monitor their WordPress instances for suspicious activity and implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent potential exploitation attempts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.description: The PixelYourSite Pro – Your smart PIXEL (TAG) Manager plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Server-Side Request Forgery in all versions up to, and including, 12.5.0.1 via the scan_video. This makes it possible for unauthenticated attackers to make web requests to arbitrary locations originating from the web application and can be used to query and modify information from internal services. The SS
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7049 is a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in the PixelYourSite Pro WordPress plugin, affecting versions up to 12.5.0.1. This vulnerability allows unauthenticated attackers to make web requests to arbitrary locations, potentially querying and modifying internal services. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, with real-world impact including potential data breaches and lateral movement within a network. To remediate, defenders should update the PixelYourSite Pro plugin to version 12.5.0.2 or later, and consider implementing network segmentation and monitoring for suspicious traffic. Additionally, defenders should review their plugin configurations to ensure that only necessary features are enabled.

items.description: The Jeg Kit for Elementor – Powerful Addons for Elementor, Widgets & Templates for WordPress plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Stored Cross-Site Scripting via the 'sg_content_number_prefix' parameter in all versions up to, and including, 3.1.0 due to insufficient input sanitization and output escaping. This makes it possible for authenticated attackers, with contributor-level access and above,
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6916 is a stored cross-site scripting vulnerability in The Jeg Kit for Elementor WordPress plugin, affecting versions up to 3.1.0. The vulnerability is caused by insufficient input sanitization and output escaping in the 'sg_content_number_prefix' parameter, allowing authenticated attackers with contributor-level access to inject malicious scripts. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access, data theft, and further malicious activities. To remediate, users should update to version 3.1.1 or later, and ensure that all input fields are properly sanitized and validated. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent potential attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-6812: The Ona theme for WordPress is vulnerable to Server-Side Request Forgery in all ...
items.description: The Ona theme for WordPress is vulnerable to Server-Side Request Forgery in all versions up to, and including, 1.26 via the ona_activate_child_theme. This makes it possible for authenticated attackers, with administrator-level access and above, to make web requests to arbitrary locations originating from the web application and can be used to query and modify information from internal services.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6812 is a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in the Ona theme for WordPress, affecting versions up to 1.26. An authenticated attacker with administrator-level access can exploit this vulnerability to make web requests to arbitrary locations, potentially querying or modifying internal services. This can lead to unauthorized data access or modification, compromising the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of sensitive information. To remediate, update the Ona theme to version 1.27 or later, and ensure that the ona_activate_child_theme function is properly sanitized to prevent SSRF attacks. Additionally, consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent such attacks.

This makes it possible for authenticated attackers, with administrator-level permissions and above, to inject arbitrary web scripts in pages that will execute whenever a user a
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6447 is a Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability in The Call for Price for WooCommerce plugin for WordPress, affecting all versions up to and including 4.2.0. This vulnerability allows authenticated attackers with administrator-level permissions to inject arbitrary web scripts, which will execute when a user accesses affected pages. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access, data tampering, or other malicious activities. To remediate, update to version 4.2.1 or later, and ensure that the 'wp-config.php' file is properly configured to prevent XSS attacks. Additionally, consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and block suspicious traffic.

items.title: GM agrees to $12.75M California settlement over sale of drivers’ data
items.description: California Attorney General Rob Bonta announced a proposed $12.75 million settlement agreement with General Motors (GM) over allegations that the company violated the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). [...]

items.title: Apple Patches Everything, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: Apple today released its typical feature update across it&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s operating systems (iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, vision OS). With this update, Apple patched 84 different vulnerabilities. Updates are available for the "26" series of operating systems, as well as for the previous "18" version of iOS/iPadOS, and two versions back for macOS (version 14 and 15).

items.title: Official CheckMarx Jenkins package compromised with infostealer
items.description: Checkmarx warned over the weekend that a rogue version of its Jenkins Application Security Testing (AST) plugin had been published on the Jenkins Marketplace. [...]

items.title: New GhostLock tool abuses Windows API to block file access
items.description: A security researcher has released a proof-of-concept tool named GhostLock that demonstrates how a legitimate Windows file API can be abused in attacks to block access to files stored locally or on SMB network shares. [...]

items.title: FCC Softens Ban on Foreign-Made Routers
items.description: The Federal Communications Commission eased some restrictions and pushed back deadlines for foreign router manufacturers, but the ban is still in place.

items.title: Texas sues Netflix over alleged data practices that create ‘surveillance machinery’ without user consent
items.description: In addition to fines, Texas is asking a judge to prevent Netflix from illegally collecting and sharing user data and to mandate that the company no longer use autoplay by default on kids’ profiles.

items.title: Tech Can't Stop These Threats — Your People Can
items.description: Security controls can do only so much. Here are four attacks where your employees are usually your first, and only, line of cyber defense.

items.title: TeamPCP Compromises Checkmarx Jenkins AST Plugin Weeks After KICS Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Checkmarx has confirmed that a modified version of the Jenkins AST plugin was published to the Jenkins Marketplace. "If you are using Checkmarx Jenkins AST plugin, you need to ensure that you are using the version 2.0.13-829.vc72453fa_1c16 that was published on December 17, 2025 or previously," the cybersecurity company said in a statement over the weekend. As of writing, Checkmarx has released

items.title: cPanel CVE-2026-41940 Under Active Exploitation to Deploy Filemanager Backdoor
items.description: A threat actor named Mr_Rot13 has been attributed to the exploitation of a recently disclosed critical cPanel flaw to deploy a backdoor codenamed Filemanager on compromised environments. The attack exploits CVE-2026-41940, a vulnerability impacting cPanel and WebHost Manager (WHM) that could result in an authentication bypass and allow remote attackers to gain elevated control of the control

items.title: Frame Security Emerges From Stealth With $50M for Awareness and Training Platform
items.description: Team8, Index Ventures, Picture Capital, Elad Gil, Cerca Partners, and Tesonet invested in Frame Security. The post Frame Security Emerges From Stealth With $50M for Awareness and Training Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FCC pushes ban on security updates for foreign-made routers, drones to 2029
items.description: The router deadline, originally slated for March 1, 2027, has been pushed back to at least January 1, 2029, according to the announcement from the FCC’s Office of Engineering and Technology (OET).

items.title: Hackers Used AI to Develop First Known Zero-Day 2FA Bypass for Mass Exploitation
items.description: Google on Monday disclosed that it identified an unknown threat actor using a zero-day exploit that it said was likely developed with an artificial intelligence (AI) system, marking the first time the technology has been put to use in the wild in a malicious context for vulnerability discovery and exploit generation. The activity is said to be the work of cybercrime threat actors who appear to

items.title: Instructure confirms hackers used Canvas flaw to deface portals
items.description: Education technology giant Instructure has confirmed that a security vulnerability allowed hackers to modify Canvas login portals and leave an extortion message. [...]

items.title: 'Dirty Frag' Exploit Poised to Blow Up on Enterprise Linux Distros
items.description: The privilege escalation vulnerability, which is similar to other Linux flaws like Copy Fail and Dirty Pipe, may already be under limited exploitation.

items.title: Why we use CAPTCHAs, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: A few months ago, I implemented Cloudflare&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Turnstile CAPTCHA on some pages. The reason for implementing these CAPTCHAs is obvious: Bots make up a large percentage of traffic and affect site performance.

items.title: Build Application Firewalls Aim to Stop the Next Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Rather than scanning code alone, Build Application Firewalls inspect runtime behavior inside the software build pipeline. The post Build Application Firewalls Aim to Stop the Next Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Why Changing Passwords Doesn’t End an Active Directory Breach
items.description: Resetting a password doesn't always remove attackers from Active Directory. Specops Software explains how cached credentials and Kerberos tickets can keep attackers authenticated after a reset. [...]

items.title: Google Detects First AI-Generated Zero-Day Exploit
items.description: The zero-day was designed to bypass 2FA and it was developed by a prominent cybercrime group. The post Google Detects First AI-Generated Zero-Day Exploit appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Google: Hackers used AI to develop zero-day exploit for web admin tool
items.description: Researchers at Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) say that a zero-day exploit targeting a popular open-source web administration tool was likely generated using AI. [...]

items.title: Hackers Use AI for Exploit Development, Attack Automation
items.description: Cyber adversaries have long used AI, but now attackers are using large language models to develop exploits and orchestrate complex attacks.

items.title: UK water company allowed hackers to lurk undetected for nearly two years, regulator finds
items.description: The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) fined South Staffordshire Water £963,900 ($1.3 million) on Monday over an attack by the Cl0p ransomware group that led to the personal data of 633,887 customers and employees being published in August 2022.

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Linux Rootkit, macOS Crypto Stealer, WebSocket Skimmers and More
items.description: Rough Monday. Somebody poisoned a trusted download again, somebody else turned cloud servers into public housing, and a few crews are still getting into boxes with bugs that should’ve died years ago — the same old holes, same lazy access paths, same “how the hell is this still open” feeling. One report this week basically reads like a guy tripped over root access by accident and decided to stay

items.title: Webinar this week: Prevention alone is not enough against modern attacks
items.description: This upcoming webinar explores how organizations need to combine security, backups, and recovery planning to reduce the impact of modern cyberattacks. [...]

items.title: Dirty Frag: Linux kernel hit by second major security flaw in two weeks
items.description: The issue was found in the same area of the Linux kernel that produced last month’s Copy Fail bug, and also allows anyone with a basic account on an affected computer to seize full administrative control.

items.title: Cyber Espionage Group Targets Aviation Firms to Steal Map Data
items.description: The campaign quietly compromises aerospace and drone operators to exfiltrate GIS files, terrain models, and GPS data and gain a clear picture of adversaries' world view.

items.title: Skoda Data Breach Hits Online Shop Customers
items.description: Using a vulnerability in the portal, hackers accessed names, addresses, email addresses, and phone numbers. The post Skoda Data Breach Hits Online Shop Customers appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Your Purple Team Isn't Purple — It's Just Red and Blue in the Same Room
items.description: Defending a network at 2 am looks a lot like this: an analyst copy-pasting a hash from a PDF into a SIEM query. A red team script is being rewritten by hand so the blue team can use it. A patch waiting on a change-approval window that's longer than the exploitation window itself. Nobody in that chain is incompetent. Every human is doing their job correctly. The problem is the system, its

items.title: Cloudflare Lays Off 1,100 Employees in AI-Driven Restructuring
items.description: The company topped revenue and earnings forecasts for the first quarter of 2026, but its shares plunged more than 20%. The post Cloudflare Lays Off 1,100 Employees in AI-Driven Restructuring appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: SailPoint Discloses GitHub Repository Hack
items.description: The incident occurred on April 20 and did not affect customer data in the company’s production and staging environments. The post SailPoint Discloses GitHub Repository Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Checkmarx Jenkins AST Plugin Compromised in Supply Chain Attack
items.description: A malicious version of the plugin was published to the Jenkins Marketplace late last week. The post Checkmarx Jenkins AST Plugin Compromised in Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: TrickMo Android banker adopts TON blockchain for covert comms
items.description: A new variant of the TrickMo Android banking malware, delivered in campaigns targeting users across Europe, introduces new commands and uses The Open Network (TON) for stealthy command-and-control communications. [...]

items.title: Canvas System Is Online After a Cyberattack Disrupted Thousands of Schools
items.description: Tens of thousands of students studying for final exams around the world have regained access to a key online learning system after a cyberattack had earlier knocked it offline. The post Canvas System Is Online After a Cyberattack Disrupted Thousands of Schools appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New ‘Dirty Frag’ Linux Vulnerability Possibly Exploited in Attacks
items.description: Also called Copy Fail 2 and tracked as CVE-2026-43284 and CVE-2026-43500, the exploit was disclosed before a patch was released. The post New ‘Dirty Frag’ Linux Vulnerability Possibly Exploited in Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Resurrected ‘Crimenetwork’ Marketplace Taken Down, Administrator Arrested
items.description: The second iteration of the German-speaking online crime marketplace had over 22,000 users and more than 100 sellers. The post Resurrected ‘Crimenetwork’ Marketplace Taken Down, Administrator Arrested appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Fake OpenAI Privacy Filter Repo Hits #1 on Hugging Face, Draws 244K Downloads
items.description: A malicious Hugging Face repository managed to take a spot in the platform's trending list by impersonating OpenAI's Privacy Filter open-weight model to deliver a Rust-based information stealer to Windows users. The project, named Open-OSS/privacy-filter, masqueraded as its legitimate counterpart released by OpenAI late last month (openai/privacy-filter), including copying the entire description

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, May 11th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9926, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: YARA-X 1.16.0 Release, (Sun, May 10th)
items.description: YARA-X&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s 1.16.0 release brings 4 improvements and 4 bugfixes.

items.title: Hackers abuse Google ads, Claude.ai chats to push Mac malware
items.description: Attackers are abusing Google Ads and legitimate Claude.ai shared chats in an active malvertising campaign. Users searching for "Claude mac download" may come across sponsored search results that list claude.ai as the target website, but lead to instructions that install malware on their Mac. [...]

items.title: Police shut down reboot of Crimenetwork marketplace, arrest admin
items.description: German authorities have shut down a relaunch version of the criminal marketplace 'Crimenetwork' that generated more than 3.6 million euros, and arrested its operator. [...]

items.title: Ollama Out-of-Bounds Read Vulnerability Allows Remote Process Memory Leak
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed a critical security vulnerability in Ollama that, if successfully exploited, could allow a remote, unauthenticated attacker to leak its entire process memory. The out-of-bounds read flaw, which likely impacts over 300,000 servers globally, is tracked as CVE-2026-7482 (CVSS score: 9.1). It has been codenamed Bleeding Llama by Cyera. Ollama is a

items.title: cPanel, WHM Release Fixes for Three New Vulnerabilities — Patch Now
items.description: cPanel has released updates to address three vulnerabilities in cPanel and Web Host Manager (WHM) that could be exploited to achieve privilege escalation, code execution, and denial-of-service. The list of vulnerabilities is as follows - CVE-2026-29201 (CVSS score: 4.3) - An insufficient input validation of the feature file name in the "feature::LOADFEATUREFILE" adminbin call that could result

items.title: ShinyHunters Claims Second Attack Against Instructure
items.description: The edtech company is struggling to wrest control from its hackers. PII belonging to hundreds of millions of people is on the line.

items.title: GM to pay over $12 million in California privacy settlement involving driver data
items.description: The settlement, announced by California officials Friday, is the largest fine issued under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) in its more than five-year history.

items.title: TCLBANKER Banking Trojan Targets Financial Platforms via WhatsApp and Outlook Worms
items.description: Threat hunters have flagged a previously undocumented Brazilian banking trojan dubbed TCLBANKER that's capable of targeting 59 banking, fintech, and cryptocurrency platforms. The activity is being tracked by Elastic Security Labs under the moniker REF3076. The malware family is assessed to be a major update of the Maverick family, which is known to leverage a worm called SORVEPOTEL to

items.title: Fake Call History Apps Stole Payments From Users After 7.3 Million Play Store Downloads
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered fraudulent apps on the official Google Play Store for Android that falsely claimed to offer access to call histories for any phone number, only to trick users into joining a subscription that provided fake data and incurred financial loss. The 28 apps have collectively racked up more than 7.3 million downloads, with one of them alone accounting for over

items.title: Another Universal Linux Local Privilege Escalation (LPE) Vulnerability: Dirty Frag, (Fri, May 8th)
items.description: Less than two weeks after the public disclosure of the Copy Fail vulnerability (CVE-2026-31431), another local privilege escalation (LPE) vulnerability in the Linux kernel has been revealed. Referred to as "Dirty Frag," this vulnerability was discovered and reported by Hyunwoo Kim (@v4bel) [1]. In this diary, I will provide a brief background on Dirty Frag, and discuss its relationship to Copy Fail. I will then discuss how to mitigate Dirty Frag and outline recommended next steps for system owners.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, May 8th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9924, (Fri, May 8th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: After Replacing TeamPCP Malware, 'PCPJack' Steals Cloud Secrets
items.description: PCPJack makes innovative use of parquet files for stealthy, pre-validated target discovery as it canvasses multiple cloud environments.

items.title: Has CISA Finally Found Its New Leader in Tom Parker?
items.description: Dark Reading investigates rumors that Tom Parker, a board room "operator" and longtime cyber exec, could be next in line to take over CISA.

items.title: AI-Driven Cyberattack on Mexico Couldn't Breach OT Systems
items.description: The most sophisticated AI-integrated campaign to date hit a brick wall in the form of a SCADA login screen.

items.title: 'TrustFall' Convention Exposes Claude Code Execution Risk
items.description: Malicious repositories can trigger code execution in Claude Code, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, and CoPilot CLI with minimal or no user interaction, thanks to skimpy warning dialogs.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, May 7th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9922, (Thu, May 7th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: An Adaptive Cyber Analytics UI for Web Honeypot Logs [Guest Diary], (Wed, May 6th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Eric Roldan, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, May 6th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9920, (Wed, May 6th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
